title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
authors: Kaiming He, Xiangyu Zhang, Shaoqing Ren, Jian Sun
abstract: Deeper neural networks are more difficult to train. We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those used previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, instead of learning unreferenced functions. Our deep residual nets achieve 3.57% error on the ImageNet test set.
content: This paper presents deep residual learning for image recognition tasks. The residual learning framework addresses the degradation problem in deep neural networks by introducing skip connections. Comprehensive experiments on ImageNet demonstrate the effectiveness of residual networks with depths of up to 152 layers.

title: Attention Is All You Need
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones
abstract: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely.
content: We propose the Transformer model architecture that eschews recurrence and instead relies entirely on an attention mechanism to draw global dependencies between input and output. Experiments on machine translation show the Transformer to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova
abstract: We introduce BERT, a new language representation model which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers.
content: BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text. The pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks without substantial task-specific architecture modifications.

title: Generative Adversarial Nets
authors: Ian Goodfellow, Jean Pouget-Abadie, Mehdi Mirza, Bing Xu, David Warde-Farley
abstract: We propose a new framework for estimating generative models via an adversarial process, in which we simultaneously train two models: a generative model G that captures the data distribution, and a discriminative model D that estimates the probability that a sample came from the training data rather than G.
content: The training procedure for G is to maximize the probability of D making a mistake. This framework corresponds to a minimax two-player game. In the space of arbitrary functions G and D, a unique solution exists with G recovering the training data distribution and D equal to 1/2 everywhere.

title: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Volodymyr Mnih, Koray Kavukcuoglu, David Silver, Alex Graves, Ioannis Antonoglou
abstract: We present the first deep learning model to successfully learn control policies directly from high-dimensional sensory input using reinforcement learning. The model is a convolutional neural network, trained with a variant of Q-learning, whose input is raw pixels and whose output is a value function estimating future rewards.
content: We apply our approach to seven Atari 2600 games from the Arcade Learning Environment, with no adjustment of the architecture or learning algorithm. We find that it outperforms all previous approaches on six of the seven games and surpasses a human expert on three of them.

title: Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning
authors: Volodymyr Mnih, Koray Kavukcuoglu, David Silver, Andrei A. Rusu, Joel Veness
abstract: An artificial agent is developed that learns to play 49 Atari 2600 games directly from the raw pixel inputs, without any hand-designed features or game-specific inductive biases. The agent uses a deep Q-network to achieve human-level performance across many challenging games.
content: This work demonstrates that a single deep reinforcement learning algorithm can achieve human-level performance across a diverse set of challenging tasks. The deep Q-network agent learns to play games by interacting with them, using only raw pixels and game score as input.

title: Mastering the Game of Go with Deep Neural Networks and Tree Search
authors: David Silver, Aja Huang, Chris J. Maddison, Arthur Guez, Laurent Sifre
abstract: The game of Go has long been viewed as the most challenging of classic games for artificial intelligence owing to its enormous search space and the difficulty of evaluating board positions. We introduce a new approach to computer Go that uses value networks to evaluate board positions and policy networks to select moves.
content: AlphaGo combines deep neural networks with Monte Carlo tree search to achieve professional-level play. The neural networks are trained by supervised learning from human expert games and by reinforcement learning from self-play. AlphaGo became the first computer program to defeat a human professional Go player.

title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, Geoffrey E. Hinton
abstract: We trained a large, deep convolutional neural network to classify the 1.2 million high-resolution images in the ImageNet LSVRC-2010 contest into the 1000 different classes. On the test data, we achieved top-1 and top-5 error rates of 37.5% and 17.0% which is considerably better than the previous state-of-the-art.
content: The neural network has 60 million parameters and 650,000 neurons, consists of five convolutional layers followed by three fully-connected layers, and employs dropout regularization. Training used stochastic gradient descent with momentum and was accelerated by GPU computing.

title: Long Short-Term Memory
authors: Sepp Hochreiter, Jurgen Schmidhuber
abstract: Learning to store information over extended time intervals by recurrent backpropagation takes a very long time, mostly due to insufficient, decaying error backflow. We propose a novel, efficient, gradient-based method called Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM).
content: LSTM introduces a memory cell and gating mechanisms that can maintain information over long periods. Truncating the gradient where it does not harm, LSTM can learn to bridge minimal time lags in excess of 1000 discrete time steps, opening up applications in sequential data processing.

title: Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting
authors: Nitish Srivastava, Geoffrey Hinton, Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, Ruslan Salakhutdinov
abstract: Deep neural nets with a large number of parameters are very powerful machine learning systems. However, overfitting is a serious problem in such networks. We show that dropout prevents overfitting and provides a way of approximately combining exponentially many different neural network architectures efficiently.
content: The key idea of dropout is to randomly drop units along with their connections from the neural network during training. This prevents units from co-adapting too much. Dropout has been shown to significantly reduce overfitting and improve performance on supervised learning tasks.

title: Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift
authors: Sergey Ioffe, Christian Szegedy
abstract: Training Deep Neural Networks is complicated by the fact that the distribution of each layer's inputs changes during training, as the parameters of the previous layers change. This slows down the training by requiring lower learning rates and careful parameter initialization.
content: We propose a new mechanism called Batch Normalization that reduces internal covariate shift by normalizing layer inputs. This allows much higher learning rates and makes training less sensitive to initialization. Batch Normalization also acts as a regularizer, reducing the need for dropout.

title: Sequence to Sequence Learning with Neural Networks
authors: Ilya Sutskever, Oriol Vinyals, Quoc V. Le
abstract: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are powerful models that have achieved excellent performance on difficult learning tasks. Although DNNs work well whenever large labeled training sets are available, they cannot be used to map sequences to sequences.
content: We present a general end-to-end approach to sequence learning that makes minimal assumptions on the sequence structure. Our method uses a multilayered LSTM to map the input sequence to a vector of a fixed dimensionality, and then another deep LSTM to decode the target sequence from the vector.

title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate
authors: Dzmitry Bahdanau, Kyunghyun Cho, Yoshua Bengio
abstract: Neural machine translation is a recently proposed approach to machine translation. Unlike the traditional statistical machine translation, the neural machine translation aims at building a single neural network that can be jointly tuned to maximize the translation performance.
content: We propose an attention mechanism that allows a model to automatically search for parts of a source sentence that are relevant to predicting a target word, without having to form a whole sentence as a fixed-length vector. This approach improves translation performance, especially for longer sentences.

title: Word2Vec: Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases and their Compositionality
authors: Tomas Mikolov, Ilya Sutskever, Kai Chen, Greg Corrado, Jeffrey Dean
abstract: The recently introduced continuous Skip-gram model is an efficient method for learning high-quality distributed vector representations that capture a large number of precise syntactic and semantic word relationships.
content: We extend the Skip-gram model to learn phrase vectors and propose sub-sampling of frequent words to speed up training. We also introduce a negative sampling algorithm that simplifies the training process. The resulting word vectors achieve state-of-the-art performance on word analogy tasks.

title: GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation
authors: Jeffrey Pennington, Richard Socher, Christopher D. Manning
abstract: Recent methods for learning vector space representations of words have succeeded in capturing fine-grained semantic and syntactic regularities using vector arithmetic. However, these methods have been developed in isolation from global matrix factorization methods.
content: We propose GloVe, a new global log-bilinear regression model that combines the advantages of global matrix factorization and local context window methods. GloVe efficiently leverages statistical information by training only on the nonzero elements of a word-word co-occurrence matrix.

title: Show, Attend and Tell: Neural Image Caption Generation with Visual Attention
authors: Kelvin Xu, Jimmy Ba, Ryan Kiros, Kyunghyun Cho, Aaron Courville
abstract: Inspired by recent work in machine translation and object detection, we propose an attention-based model that automatically learns to describe the content of images. We describe how we can train this model in a deterministic manner using standard backpropagation techniques.
content: Our model learns to attend to salient objects while generating captions. We visualize the attention weights to show which regions of the image the model focuses on during caption generation. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on the Flickr8k, Flickr30k and MS COCO datasets.

title: Fast R-CNN
authors: Ross Girshick
abstract: This paper proposes a Fast Region-based Convolutional Network method (Fast R-CNN) for object detection. Fast R-CNN builds on previous work to efficiently classify object proposals using deep convolutional networks.
content: Fast R-CNN employs several innovations to improve training and testing speed while also increasing detection accuracy. It uses a single-stage training algorithm that jointly learns to classify object proposals and refine their spatial locations.

title: Faster R-CNN: Towards Real-Time Object Detection with Region Proposal Networks
authors: Shaoqing Ren, Kaiming He, Ross Girshick, Jian Sun
abstract: State-of-the-art object detection networks depend on region proposal algorithms to hypothesize object locations. Advances like SPPnet and Fast R-CNN have reduced the running time of these detection networks, exposing region proposal computation as a bottleneck.
content: We introduce a Region Proposal Network (RPN) that shares full-image convolutional features with the detection network, enabling nearly cost-free region proposals. RPN is a fully convolutional network that simultaneously predicts object bounds and objectness scores at each position.

title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
authors: Joseph Redmon, Santosh Divvala, Ross Girshick, Ali Farhadi
abstract: We present YOLO, a new approach to object detection. Prior work on object detection repurposes classifiers to perform detection. Instead, we frame object detection as a regression problem to spatially separated bounding boxes and associated class probabilities.
content: A single neural network predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities directly from full images in one evaluation. Since the whole detection pipeline is a single network, it can be optimized end-to-end directly on detection performance. YOLO runs at 45 frames per second on a GPU.

title: Mask R-CNN
authors: Kaiming He, Georgia Gkioxari, Piotr Dollar, Ross Girshick
abstract: We present a conceptually simple, flexible, and general framework for object instance segmentation. Our approach efficiently detects objects in an image while simultaneously generating a high-quality segmentation mask for each instance.
content: Mask R-CNN extends Faster R-CNN by adding a branch for predicting segmentation masks on each Region of Interest, in parallel with the existing branch for bounding box recognition. The method also generalizes well to other tasks like human pose estimation.

title: Graph Attention Networks
authors: Petar Velickovic, Guillem Cucurull, Arantxa Casanova, Adriana Romero, Pietro Lio
abstract: We present graph attention networks (GATs), novel neural network architectures that operate on graph-structured data, leveraging masked self-attentional layers to address the shortcomings of prior graph convolution based methods.
content: GATs compute hidden representations of each node by attending over its neighbors, following a self-attention strategy. The attention mechanism enables specifying different weights to different nodes in a neighborhood, handling heterogeneous degree distributions effectively.

title: Variational Autoencoders
authors: Diederik P. Kingma, Max Welling
abstract: How can we perform efficient inference and learning in directed probabilistic models with continuous latent variables and intractable posterior distributions? We introduce a stochastic variational inference and learning algorithm that scales to large datasets.
content: The Variational Autoencoder (VAE) framework uses a recognition model to approximate the intractable posterior and a generative model to reconstruct data. The VAE objective is a variational lower bound that can be optimized using stochastic gradient descent.

title: Siamese Neural Networks for One-shot Image Recognition
authors: Gregory Koch, Richard Zemel, Ruslan Salakhutdinov
abstract: Learning to recognize novel categories from only a few examples is a significant challenge for machine learning systems. In this work we employ siamese neural networks to address the one-shot learning problem for image recognition.
content: Our siamese network learns a distance metric that generalizes well to unseen categories. We demonstrate competitive one-shot classification performance on the Omniglot dataset, approaching human-level accuracy with only a single example per novel class.

title: Pointer Networks
authors: Oriol Vinyals, Meire Fortunato, Navdeep Jaitly
abstract: We introduce a new neural architecture to learn the conditional probability of an output sequence with elements that are discrete tokens corresponding to positions in an input sequence. Such problems cannot be trivially addressed by existing approaches like sequence-to-sequence.
content: Our Pointer Network model uses attention as a pointer to select a member of the input sequence as the output. We show its effectiveness on three challenging geometric problems: finding convex hulls, Delaunay triangulations, and the traveling salesman problem.

title: Dueling Network Architectures for Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ziyu Wang, Tom Schaul, Matteo Hessel, Hado van Hasselt, Marc Lanctot
abstract: In recent years there have been many successes of using deep representations in reinforcement learning. Still, many of these applications use conventional architectures such as convolutional networks, LSTMs, or auto-encoders.
content: We present a new neural network architecture for model-free reinforcement learning. The dueling network represents two separate estimators: one for the state value function and one for the state-dependent action advantage function. Our approach leads to better policy evaluation.

title: Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms
authors: John Schulman, Filip Wolski, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alec Radford, Oleg Klimov
abstract: We propose a new family of policy gradient methods for reinforcement learning, which alternate between sampling data through interaction with the environment and optimizing a surrogate objective function using stochastic gradient ascent.
content: PPO algorithms have some of the benefits of trust region policy optimization but are much simpler to implement, more general, and have better sample complexity. Our experiments show PPO outperforms other online policy gradient methods on a suite of benchmark tasks.

title: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision
authors: Alec Radford, Jong Wook Kim, Chris Hallacy, Aditya Ramesh, Gabriel Goh
abstract: State-of-the-art computer vision systems are trained to predict a fixed set of predetermined object categories. This restricted form of supervision limits their generality and usability since additional labeled data is needed to specify any other visual concept.
content: We present CLIP, a model that learns visual representations from natural language supervision. CLIP is pre-trained on 400 million image-text pairs and can be adapted to perform a wide variety of classification benchmarks without requiring dataset-specific training.

title: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
authors: Alexey Dosovitskiy, Lucas Beyer, Alexander Kolesnikov, Dirk Weissenborn, Xiaohua Zhai
abstract: While the Transformer architecture has become the de-facto standard for natural language processing tasks, its application to computer vision remains limited. In vision, attention is either applied in conjunction with convolutional networks or used to replace certain components.
content: We show that a pure Transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches can perform very well on image classification tasks. The Vision Transformer (ViT) attains excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks while requiring substantially fewer computational resources to train.

title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan
abstract: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even becoming competitive with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. We train GPT-3, a 175 billion parameter autoregressive language model.
content: GPT-3 achieves strong performance on many NLP datasets, including translation, question answering, and cloze tasks, as well as several tasks that require on-the-fly reasoning or domain adaptation. We also discuss the broader societal impacts of such powerful language models.

title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback
authors: Long Ouyang, Jeff Wu, Xu Jiang, Diogo Almeida, Carroll L. Wainwright
abstract: Making language models bigger does not inherently make them better at following a user's intent. We show an avenue for aligning language models with user intent across a wide variety of tasks by fine-tuning with human feedback.
content: We use reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to fine-tune GPT-3 to follow instructions. The resulting InstructGPT models are significantly better than GPT-3 at following English instructions while showing reductions in toxic outputs.

title: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models
authors: Jonathan Ho, Ajay Jain, Pieter Abbeel
abstract: We present high quality image synthesis results using diffusion probabilistic models, a class of latent variable models inspired by considerations from nonequilibrium thermodynamics.
content: Our diffusion model achieves excellent sample quality and log-likelihood scores. We show that diffusion models can generate high-fidelity images competitive with GANs, while providing better coverage of the data distribution and more stable training.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Sports Analytics: A Survey
authors: Michael Chen, Sarah Johnson, David Park
abstract: Reinforcement learning has emerged as a powerful tool for sports analytics, enabling dynamic strategy optimization and player evaluation. This survey covers applications in basketball, baseball, soccer, and American football.
content: We review recent advances in applying RL to sports including play calling optimization, player tracking, and injury prediction. The survey identifies key challenges including sparse reward structures and the difficulty of simulating realistic game dynamics.

title: Deep Learning for Healthcare: Review and Future Directions
authors: Rahul Gupta, Priya Sharma, Emily Zhang
abstract: Deep learning is transforming healthcare through improved diagnosis, treatment planning, and drug discovery. This review examines the current state of deep learning applications in clinical settings and identifies promising directions for future research.
content: We analyze 500+ papers on deep learning in healthcare covering medical imaging, electronic health records, genomics, and drug discovery. Key challenges include data privacy, model interpretability, and the need for prospective clinical validation.

title: Explainable AI: A Survey of Methods and Applications
authors: Alexandre Torres, Ricardo Santos, Maria Rodriguez
abstract: Interpretability and explainability are crucial for building trust in AI systems, especially in high-stakes domains such as healthcare, finance, and criminal justice. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of XAI methods.
content: We categorize XAI methods into intrinsic and post-hoc approaches, and further subdivide by scope (global vs local) and model specificity (model-specific vs model-agnostic). We also discuss evaluation metrics and identify gaps in current XAI research.

title: Federated Learning: Challenges, Methods, and Future Directions
authors: Tian Li, Anit Kumar Sahu, Ameet Talwalkar, Virginia Smith
abstract: Federated learning involves training statistical models over remote devices or siloed data centers while keeping data locally. This approach addresses fundamental problems of privacy, security, and data governance.
content: We discuss the unique characteristics and challenges of federated learning, including communication efficiency, statistical heterogeneity, and privacy guarantees. We provide a comprehensive overview of state-of-the-art methods and open problems in the field.

title: Self-Supervised Learning: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Liang Jing, Yann LeCun, Ishan Misra
abstract: Self-supervised learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for learning representations from unlabeled data. This review provides a comprehensive overview of self-supervised learning methods across computer vision, NLP, and multimodal domains.
content: We cover contrastive methods, masked image modeling, and generative approaches to self-supervised learning. The review also discusses theoretical foundations, evaluation protocols, and the role of data augmentation in self-supervised representation learning.

title: Transformer-based Models for Time Series Forecasting: A Survey
authors: Qingsong Wen, Tian Zhou, Chaoli Zhang, Weiqi Chen, Ziqing Ma
abstract: Time series forecasting is a critical task in many domains including finance, weather, and energy. Transformer-based models have recently achieved state-of-the-art results across various time series benchmarks.
content: We survey the adaptation of Transformer architectures for time series, covering positional encoding schemes, attention mechanisms, and architectural variants. We identify challenges including computational complexity and handling of long-range dependencies.

title: AI in Education: Personalized Learning Paths
authors: Jennifer Martinez, Kevin O'Brien, Anna Kowalski
abstract: Artificial intelligence offers unprecedented opportunities for personalized education by adapting learning content to individual student needs. This paper presents a framework for AI-driven personalized learning path generation.
content: Our framework combines knowledge tracing, recommender systems, and natural language processing to create adaptive learning experiences. Experiments in a large-scale deployment show significant improvements in student engagement and learning outcomes.

title: Graph Neural Networks for Molecular Property Prediction
authors: Zhaoping Xiong, Dingyan Wang, Xiaohong Liu, Feisheng Zhong, Xiaozhe Wan
source: Journal of Chemical Information and Modeling
abstract: Molecular property prediction is a fundamental problem in drug discovery and materials science. Graph neural networks have emerged as a powerful approach for learning from molecular graph structures.
content: We benchmark various GNN architectures on molecular property prediction tasks and identify key design choices including message-passing schemes and readout functions. We also discuss the role of 3D molecular geometry in graph-based molecular modeling.

title: Neural Radiance Fields for 3D Scene Representation
authors: Ben Mildenhall, Pratul P. Srinivasan, Matthew Tancik, Jonathan T. Barron, Ravi Ramamoorthi
abstract: We propose a neural radiance field (NeRF) representation for view synthesis of complex scenes with only a sparse set of input views. Our method optimizes a continuous volumetric scene function using a fully-connected deep network.
content: NeRF represents a static scene as a 5D continuous function that outputs the radiance emitted in each direction at each point in space. Novel views are rendered by marching along camera rays and accumulating color and density values.

title: Large Language Models for Code Generation: A Survey
authors: Yao Wan, Zhou Zhao, Min Yang, Guandong Xu, Hongyu Zhang
abstract: Large language models have shown remarkable capabilities in generating code from natural language descriptions. This survey provides a systematic review of LLM-based code generation methods, benchmarks, and applications.
content: We cover prompting strategies, fine-tuning approaches, and retrieval-augmented generation for code. We analyze performance on benchmarks like HumanEval and MBPP and discuss challenges including hallucination, security, and evaluation methodology.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning: A Survey of Recent Advances
authors: Kaiqing Zhang, Zhuoran Yang, Tamer Basar
abstract: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) addresses sequential decision-making problems with multiple agents interacting in a shared environment. This survey reviews recent algorithmic advances in MARL.
content: We cover value-based methods, policy gradient approaches, mean-field methods, and communication-based MARL. Key challenges include non-stationarity, scalability, and the need for centralized training with decentralized execution.

title: Ethical Considerations in Large Language Model Development
authors: Margaret Mitchell, Emily M. Bender, Timnit Gebru, Angelina McMillan-Major
abstract: Large language models raise significant ethical concerns including bias, fairness, environmental impact, and potential for misuse. This paper provides a framework for responsible LLM development and deployment.
content: We discuss documentation practices, pre-deployment auditing, and ongoing monitoring of LLM systems. We argue that model cards, data statements, and impact assessments should be standard practice for all large-scale language model releases.

title: Efficient Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models with LoRA
authors: Edward Hu, Yelong Shen, Phillip Wallis, Zeyuan Allen-Zhu, Yuanzhi Li
abstract: Full fine-tuning of large language models is prohibitively expensive in terms of compute and memory. We propose Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) which freezes the pre-trained weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices.
content: LoRA achieves comparable performance to full fine-tuning while reducing the number of trainable parameters by 10,000x and GPU memory requirements by 3x. Our approach has no additional inference latency and can be combined with other methods like prefix tuning.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
authors: Patrick Lewis, Ethan Perez, Aleksandra Piktus, Fabio Petroni, Vladimir Karpukhin
abstract: Large pre-trained language models have been shown to store factual knowledge in their parameters, but their ability to access and precisely manipulate knowledge remains limited. We introduce RAG models that combine pre-trained parametric and non-parametric memory.
content: Our RAG architecture uses a dense passage retriever to retrieve relevant documents from a knowledge corpus and a sequence-to-sequence model to generate answers conditioned on retrieved content. RAG achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple knowledge-intensive tasks.

title: Diffusion Models for Video Generation: A Survey
authors: Lijun Yu, Yong Cheng, Kihyuk Sohn, Jose Lezama, Han Zhang
abstract: Diffusion models have become the dominant approach for image generation and are now being extended to video generation. This survey covers the adaptation of diffusion models to the temporal domain for video synthesis.
content: We review approaches including spatio-temporal UNet architectures, latent video diffusion, and cascaded generation pipelines. We discuss challenges such as temporal consistency, computational cost, and evaluation metrics for video generation.

title: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback: Progress and Challenges
authors: Yuntao Bai, Andy Jones, Kamal Ndousse, Amanda Askell, Anna Chen
abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has emerged as a key technique for aligning language models with human preferences. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of RLHF methods and open challenges.
content: We cover the three-stage RLHF pipeline: supervised fine-tuning, reward model training, and policy optimization. We also discuss alternatives to RLHF including direct preference optimization and constitutional AI approaches.

title: Transformers in Medical Image Analysis: A Survey
authors: Jieneng Chen, Yongyi Lu, Qihang Yu, Xiangde Luo, Ehsan Adeli
abstract: Transformer architectures have achieved remarkable success in medical image analysis tasks including segmentation, detection, and classification. This survey provides a systematic review of transformer-based methods in medical imaging.
content: We analyze over 200 papers applying transformers to medical imaging, covering architecture designs, pre-training strategies, and domain-specific adaptations. Key challenges include limited medical datasets and the need for hybrid CNN-Transformer models.

title: Neural Architecture Search: A Survey
authors: Thomas Elsken, Jan Hendrik Metzen, Frank Hutter
abstract: Neural architecture search (NAS) aims to automate the design of neural network architectures. This survey provides an overview of the state of the art in NAS methods, search spaces, and optimization strategies.
content: We categorize NAS methods into reinforcement learning-based, evolutionary, and gradient-based approaches. We discuss how NAS has produced architectures that surpass manually designed networks on image classification and other tasks.

title: Machine Learning for Climate Change: A Comprehensive Review
authors: David Rolnick, Priya L. Donti, Lynn H. Kaack, Kelly Kochanski, Alexis Lacoste
abstract: Machine learning can play a critical role in addressing climate change across mitigation, adaptation, and climate science. This review maps ML applications to climate challenges and identifies high-impact opportunities.
content: We identify 12 high-impact application areas spanning energy systems, transportation, agriculture, and climate modeling. The review also discusses cross-cutting challenges including data availability, model interpretability, and equity considerations.

title: Contrastive Learning for Multimodal Representations
authors: Yuan Gong, Yu-An Chung, James Glass
abstract: Learning effective representations from multimodal data is challenging due to the heterogeneous nature of different modalities. We propose a contrastive learning framework for aligning representations across modalities.
content: Our framework maximizes mutual information between paired multimodal samples while minimizing it between unpaired ones. We demonstrate strong performance on cross-modal retrieval and multimodal classification tasks.

title: Tabular Data Learning: Current Status and Future Directions
authors: Vadim Borisov, Tobias Leemann, Kathrin Seßler, Johannes Haug, Martin Pawelczyk
abstract: Tabular data remains one of the most common data formats in machine learning applications. This survey examines the current state of deep learning methods for tabular data compared to traditional tree-based methods.
content: We benchmark deep learning approaches including TabNet, FT-Transformer, and NODE against XGBoost and LightGBM. Results show tree-based methods still dominate on medium-sized datasets, but deep learning shows promise for very large datasets.

title: Adversarial Attacks and Defenses in Deep Learning: From Image to Text
authors: Wei Emma Zhang, Quan Z. Sheng, Ahoud Alhazmi, Chenliang Li
abstract: Deep neural networks are vulnerable to adversarial examples—inputs with imperceptible perturbations that cause misclassification. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of adversarial attacks and defenses.
content: We cover threat models, attack methods (white-box, black-box, and physical attacks), and defense strategies (adversarial training, gradient masking, and certified defenses). We also discuss adversarial robustness for NLP models.

title: Robotics and Automation in Healthcare: A Review of Current Applications
authors: Amanda Watson, Robert Kim, Lisa Chen
abstract: Robotics and automation technologies are increasingly being deployed in healthcare settings, from surgical robots to automated pharmacy systems. This review examines current applications and future opportunities.
content: We analyze robotic systems in surgery, rehabilitation, laboratory automation, and hospital logistics. Key trends include AI-enhanced robotic control, human-robot interaction, and the integration of autonomous mobile robots in hospital environments.

title: Bayesian Deep Learning: A Survey
authors: Laurence Aitchison, James Allingham, Eric Nalisnick, Andrew Gordon Wilson
abstract: Bayesian deep learning combines the flexibility of deep neural networks with principled uncertainty quantification. This survey provides an overview of methods, applications, and open challenges in Bayesian deep learning.
content: We cover variational inference, Monte Carlo dropout, deep ensembles, and Laplace approximation methods. We discuss applications in active learning, Bayesian optimization, and safety-critical systems requiring reliable uncertainty estimates.
